title: Introduction to Computer Systems
summary: Overview of what computers are, their history, and how binary information is processed.
content_html:
A computer system is a machine that processes information. At its core, every computer represents data as binary digits (bits) — values of 0 or 1.

Computers process information through three fundamental operations: representation (encoding data as bits), manipulation (applying logic and arithmetic), and storage (holding data in memory).

Key Historical Milestones

  • Charles Babbage (1791–1871) — Designed mechanical computing engines
  • Claude Shannon (1937) — Showed Boolean algebra can describe circuits
  • John von Neumann (1945) — Defined the stored-program architecture still used today

Major Computing Eras

  • Batch processing (1950s–60s): Jobs queued on punch cards
  • Time-sharing (1960s–70s): Multiple users, one CPU
  • Personal Computing (1980s): Computers in every home
  • Networking / Internet (1990s–2000s)
  • Cloud & Edge Computing (2010s–present)

Moore's Law

Computing power approximately doubles every 2 years, as observed by Gordon Moore. This exponential growth has driven rapid advances in hardware capability over decades.

The Unit COS10004

In this unit you will: read and write ARM assembly programs, identify hardware components and their roles, and understand how compilers, assemblers, and linkers work together.

exercises.title: Information operations
exercises.prompt: Name the three fundamental operations a computer performs on information.

exercises.title: Claude Shannon
exercises.prompt: What was Claude Shannon's key contribution to computer systems?
options: Showed Boolean algebra can describe electrical circuits
exercises.answer: Showed Boolean algebra can describe electrical circuits

exercises.title: Byte size
exercises.prompt: How many bits are in one byte?

exercises.title: Binary representation
exercises.prompt: Why do computers use binary (0s and 1s) rather than decimal?
options: Binary is simple, robust, and easy to implement with electronic circuits
Humans designed computers to use binary
exercises.answer: Binary is simple, robust, and easy to implement with electronic circuits

exercises.title: Historical era
exercises.prompt: Which computing era is described as 'many users sharing one CPU'?

exercises.title: Unit outcomes

exercises.title: Word size
exercises.prompt: What is a 'word' in computer architecture?
options: The register size used by the CPU (8, 16, 32, or 64 bits)
exercises.answer: The register size used by the CPU (8, 16, 32, or 64 bits)
